draft.introduction: The Lord’s Prayer in Matthew’s Sermon on the Mount is the most recited prayer in Christianity. It condenses the entire Gospel into seven petitions addressed to “Our Father in heaven.”

translation.text: Pray then like this: Our Father in heaven, hallowed be your name. Your kingdom come, your will be done, on earth as it is in heaven. Give us this day our daily bread, and forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors. And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil.
translation.original_text: πάτερ ἡμῶν ὁ ἐν τοῖς οὐρανοῖς ἁγιασθήτω τὸ ὄνομά σου

translation.text: For if you forgive others their trespasses, your heavenly Father will also forgive you, but if you do not forgive others their trespasses, neither will your Father forgive your trespasses.

translator_notes.note: The word ἐπιούσιον (“daily”) appears nowhere else in Greek literature before this prayer. Its meaning is debated: “for the coming day,” “supersubstantial” (Jerome’s rendering), or “necessary for existence.” The ambiguity may be intentional.
